attack_paths.title: Cloud/account pivot: server access → harvested secret
attack_paths.impact: Compromise of external systems the leaked credential unlocks
attack_paths.advice: Break the chain at step 1 — parameterise the query / stop running attacker-controlled input (app/api/products/route.ts:7). Fixing that one thing stops this whole path.
score_factors: chain reaches a concrete impact (data/system compromise)
exposes a credential — blast radius beyond this app
2 distinct weaknesses align into one chain

steps.title: Server-side code/query execution
steps.narrative: User input flows into a database query (app/api/products/route.ts:7) can run code or queries on the server.

steps.title: A real secret is sitting right there
steps.narrative: AWS access key committed to the repository (.env.local:2) puts a live credential within reach of that primitive.

steps.title: Pivot beyond the app with stolen keys
steps.narrative: With the secret in hand, the attacker authenticates to your cloud / database / third-party services — the blast radius now extends past this app.

attack_paths.title: Build/CI compromise via a malicious dependency
attack_paths.impact: Code execution on your build server, CI, or developers' machines
attack_paths.advice: Break the chain at step 1 — remove or pin this dependency and audit the script (package.json:8). Fixing that one thing stops this whole path.
score_factors: chain reaches a concrete impact (data/system compromise)

steps.title: Malicious code arrives via a dependency
steps.narrative: Dangerous install lifecycle script in package.json (package.json:8) — Lifecycle script 'postinstall': Pipes remote content directly into a shell/interpreter.

steps.title: It runs at install time, before your app starts
steps.narrative: npm runs lifecycle scripts on `npm install` / CI — this executes on your build server or developer machine with no app code involved.

attack_paths.title: Injection via untrusted input → paint() -> el.innerHTML [in lib/render.ts]
attack_paths.advice: Break the chain at step 2 — validate or parameterise the value at the sink (app/api/products/route.ts:10). Fixing that one thing stops this whole path.
score_factors: chain reaches a concrete impact (data/system compromise)

steps.title: Attacker controls untrusted input
steps.narrative: An attacker supplies a value via `req.body` (app/api/products/route.ts:5) reaching app/api/products/route.ts. Nothing about this input is trusted.

steps.title: Untrusted input reaches a dangerous sink
steps.narrative: That value flows unsanitised into `paint() -> el.innerHTML [in lib/render.ts]` (app/api/products/route.ts:10) — the attacker now controls what the server runs.

attack_paths.title: Exploitation of lodash via CVE-2021-23337
attack_paths.impact: Compromise via a publicly-known dependency exploit
attack_paths.advice: Break the chain at step 1 — upgrade lodash to 4.17.21 (package.json:14). Fixing that one thing stops this whole path.
score_factors: chain reaches a concrete impact (data/system compromise)

steps.title: Exposed dependency with a public exploit
steps.narrative: You ship `lodash` affected by CVE-2021-23337, reachable from code you actually call.

steps.title: Attacker triggers the known vulnerability
steps.narrative: Public exploit code exists; the attacker runs it against your deployment.

findings.message: Looks like a AWS Access Key ID.
findings.title: AWS access key committed to the repository
findings.why: An AWS access key pair grants programmatic access to your cloud account. Committed AWS keys are scraped automatically and can be used to spin up servers (huge bills) or read your data within minutes of being pushed.
findings.fix: Deactivate and delete this key in the AWS IAM console immediately, then issue a new one and store it in your host's secret manager / environment — never in code.
findings.secure_example: // load from process.env at runtime; configure via AWS IAM roles where possible

findings.message: User input from `req.body` flows into searchProducts() -> client.query(...) [in lib/db.ts].
findings.title: User input flows into a database query
findings.why:
NjordScan traced a value that started from user input (a request body, query
string, route param, etc.) all the way into a database call — `.query()`,
`.execute()`, a Prisma `$queryRawUnsafe`/`$executeRawUnsafe`, or a `.raw()`
query — without it being parameterized along the way. Because the value lands
in the *query text*, an attacker can inject their own SQL: read other users'
rows, bypass a login with `OR 1=1 --`, or `DROP TABLE`. This is SQL injection
proven by data flow, not just a suspicious-looking string.
findings.fix:
Pass user data as parameters, never as part of the query text. Use placeholders
(`?` / `$1`) with a values array, or an ORM/query-builder (Prisma's typed
client, Drizzle, Knex's binding API). For Prisma specifically, prefer the safe
tagged-template `$queryRaw`/`$executeRaw` (which parameterizes `${...}`) over
the `...Unsafe` variants. Validate/allow-list anything that must be part of the
SQL structure itself (table/column names), since those can't be parameterized.

findings.code_snippet: return client.query('SELECT * FROM products WHERE name LIKE %' + term + '%');
findings.message: SQL query is concatenated with a variable — a user can inject their own SQL. Use a parameterized query with ? / $1 placeholders.
findings.title: SQL query built by glueing strings together
findings.why:
When you build a SQL query by adding (`+`) a variable into the query
string, the database can't tell your query apart from data the user typed.
If that variable came from a form, a URL, or an API request, an attacker can
type SQL of their own — for example ending your query early and adding
`OR 1=1 --` to dump every row, or `; DROP TABLE users; --` to delete data.
This is the classic "SQL injection" bug, and it can leak or destroy your
entire database.
findings.fix:
Never put user data directly into the query text. Use a parameterized query
(also called a "prepared statement"): write `?` or `$1` placeholders and
pass the values as a separate array. The database driver then treats them as
pure data that can never become SQL. If you use an ORM (Prisma, Drizzle,
Sequelize), use its query builder instead of raw string SQL.
findings.secure_example:
// instead of: db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ' + id)
db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', [id]); // value is sent separately

findings.message: Lifecycle script 'postinstall': Pipes remote content directly into a shell/interpreter.
findings.title: Dangerous install lifecycle script in package.json
findings.why: `preinstall`/`postinstall` scripts run automatically on `npm install`, with your user's permissions. A script that pipes a remote file into a shell (curl | sh), opens a reverse shell, or reads credentials is a classic supply-chain attack — it runs before you ever start your app.
findings.fix: Review the script and the package that ships it. Install untrusted packages with `npm install --ignore-scripts`. Remove any install script that fetches and executes remote code.
findings.secure_example: npm install --ignore-scripts   # then run only the build steps you trust

findings.message: Looks like a Database URL with credentials.
findings.title: Hard-coded secret or credential
findings.why: A secret committed to your code (API key, password, token) is visible to anyone who can see the repository — and stays in git history forever, even if you delete the line later. Leaked keys are routinely scraped from public repos within minutes and used to run up bills or steal data.
findings.fix: Move the value to an environment variable, add the env file to .gitignore, and ROTATE the exposed secret now (assume it is already compromised). On Next.js, only expose values to the browser via the NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix when you intend them to be public.
findings.secure_example: const apiKey = process.env.API_KEY; // set in your host's env / .env.local (gitignored)

findings.message: `NEXT_PUBLIC_INTERNAL_API_TOKEN` is exposed to the browser bundle.
findings.title: Secret exposed to the browser via NEXT_PUBLIC_ / VITE_
findings.why: Variables prefixed with NEXT_PUBLIC_ (Next.js) or VITE_ (Vite) are inlined into the JavaScript bundle that ships to every visitor. Putting a real secret (private API key, DB password) behind that prefix publishes it to the whole world.
findings.fix: Only use the public prefix for values that are safe to be public. Keep real secrets unprefixed and read them only in server code (Route Handlers, Server Actions, getServerSideProps).
findings.secure_example:
// server only:
const key = process.env.STRIPE_SECRET_KEY; // NOT NEXT_PUBLIC_

findings.message: This AI route calls the model but the file has no sign-in/auth check — anyone can hit it and run up your AI bill (denial of wallet). Require a logged-in user before the model call.
findings.title: AI route calls the model with no sign-in / auth check in the file
findings.why:
This route handler calls a paid AI model (OpenAI / Anthropic / etc.) but
nothing in the file checks who is calling it — no session, no login, no API
token. That means ANYONE on the internet who finds the URL can hit it as
many times as they like. Every call costs you money, so a bot can run up a
huge bill on your account overnight ("denial of wallet"), and your model
quota gets drained for real users. It also lets strangers use your app as a
free LLM proxy. (This is a heuristic: if your auth check lives in middleware
or a shared helper this file doesn't reference, you can add a
`// njordscan-ignore` comment on the call line.)
findings.fix:
Before calling the model, confirm the request is from a logged-in user and
reject it otherwise. Use whatever auth you already have: `getServerSession`
(NextAuth), `auth()` (Clerk), `supabase.auth.getUser()`, or a check on the
`Authorization` header / API key for server-to-server calls. Return 401 if
the check fails — do this first, before any model call.

findings.code_snippet: document.getElementById('a').innerHTML = answer; // LLM output rendered as HTML (XSS)
findings.message: innerHTML assigned a non-literal value; renders as HTML and can execute injected scripts if user-controlled.
findings.title: User input assigned to innerHTML / outerHTML
findings.why: Assigning a string to `element.innerHTML` parses it as HTML. When the string contains data an attacker controls, they can inject markup that runs JavaScript in your users' browsers (cross-site scripting), letting them hijack accounts or steal data.
findings.fix: Use `element.textContent` to insert text safely, or build DOM nodes explicitly. In React, render `{value}` instead of touching innerHTML. If raw HTML is unavoidable, sanitize with DOMPurify first.
findings.secure_example: element.textContent = userInput; // rendered as text, never executed

findings.message: User input from `req.body` flows into paint() -> el.innerHTML [in lib/render.ts].
findings.title: User input assigned to innerHTML / outerHTML
findings.why: Assigning a string to `element.innerHTML` parses it as HTML. When the string contains data an attacker controls, they can inject markup that runs JavaScript in your users' browsers (cross-site scripting), letting them hijack accounts or steal data.
findings.fix: Use `element.textContent` to insert text safely, or build DOM nodes explicitly. In React, render `{value}` instead of touching innerHTML. If raw HTML is unavoidable, sanitize with DOMPurify first.
findings.secure_example: element.textContent = userInput; // rendered as text, never executed

findings.message: innerHTML assigned a non-literal value; renders as HTML and can execute injected scripts if user-controlled.
findings.title: User input assigned to innerHTML / outerHTML
findings.why: Assigning a string to `element.innerHTML` parses it as HTML. When the string contains data an attacker controls, they can inject markup that runs JavaScript in your users' browsers (cross-site scripting), letting them hijack accounts or steal data.
findings.fix: Use `element.textContent` to insert text safely, or build DOM nodes explicitly. In React, render `{value}` instead of touching innerHTML. If raw HTML is unavoidable, sanitize with DOMPurify first.
findings.secure_example: element.textContent = userInput; // rendered as text, never executed

findings.message: lodash@4.17.15 is affected by CVE-2021-23337 (vulnerable <4.17.21). Upgrade to 4.17.21 or later. 🎯 Reachable: your code calls the vulnerable `template` from `lodash`. Command injection in lodash via the template function: untrusted templates/options can execute arbitrary code.
findings.title: Dependency with a known security vulnerability
findings.why: This package version has a publicly documented security flaw (a CVE/advisory). Attackers actively scan for apps using vulnerable versions because a working exploit is already published.
findings.fix: Upgrade to the patched version listed in the advisory (`npm update <pkg>` or bump it in package.json).

metadata.summary: Command injection in lodash via the template function: untrusted templates/options can execute arbitrary code.

findings.message: dangerouslySetInnerHTML receives a non-literal value; if any part is user-controlled this is an XSS sink.
findings.title: Untrusted data rendered with dangerouslySetInnerHTML
findings.why: React normally escapes everything you render, which protects your users automatically. `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` turns that protection OFF and injects raw HTML. If any part of that HTML comes from a user (a form field, a URL, an API response, the database), an attacker can smuggle in a <script> tag and run their own JavaScript in your users' browsers — stealing logins, cookies, or making requests as the victim. This is called cross-site scripting (XSS).
findings.fix: Prefer rendering text as `{value}` and let React escape it. If you truly need to render HTML (e.g. rich text from a CMS), sanitize it first with a library like DOMPurify and only then pass the cleaned string to dangerouslySetInnerHTML.

findings.message: This AI route calls the model with no rate limit in the file — one bot can call it thousands of times and drain your AI budget. Add a per-user/IP limit (e.g. Upstash Ratelimit) before the model call.
findings.title: AI route calls the model with no rate limit in the file
findings.why:
This route calls a paid AI model but nothing in the file limits how often a
single user (or a bot) can call it. AI calls cost real money per request, so
without a cap one abusive client can hammer the endpoint thousands of times
and run up your bill or exhaust your model quota for everyone else ("denial
of wallet"). Even a friendly bug — a retry loop in the UI — can do this by
accident. (Heuristic: if you rate-limit in middleware or a shared helper
this file doesn't reference, add `// njordscan-ignore` on the call line.)
findings.fix:
Add a per-user / per-IP rate limit before the model call. A common setup is
Upstash Ratelimit on Vercel, but any limiter works. Reject requests that go
over the limit with HTTP 429 before spending money on a model call.

findings.message: next.config defines no security headers (no `headers()` setting X-Frame-Options / Content-Security-Policy / Strict-Transport-Security). Add them for defense in depth, unless you set them in middleware.
findings.title: Missing security response headers
findings.why: Security headers (Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Content-Type-Options) are extra guardrails the browser enforces for you — blocking clickjacking, forcing HTTPS, and limiting the damage of an XSS bug. Without them you lose defense in depth.
findings.fix: Set security headers in next.config.js `headers()` (or your server/middleware).

findings.code_snippet: typescript: { ignoreBuildErrors: true },   // ships type errors to prod
findings.message: `typescript.ignoreBuildErrors: true` disables a safety check, letting type/lint errors ship to production.
findings.title: Insecure Next.js configuration
findings.why: Some next.config options loosen safety in ways that are easy to ship by accident — e.g. ignoring build/type errors hides real bugs, and overly broad image `domains`/`remotePatterns` let attackers proxy arbitrary content through your domain.
findings.fix: Don't ignore type/lint errors in production builds, and scope image and rewrite patterns to hosts you control.

findings.message: `eslint.ignoreDuringBuilds: true` disables a safety check, letting type/lint errors ship to production.
findings.title: Insecure Next.js configuration
findings.why: Some next.config options loosen safety in ways that are easy to ship by accident — e.g. ignoring build/type errors hides real bugs, and overly broad image `domains`/`remotePatterns` let attackers proxy arbitrary content through your domain.
findings.fix: Don't ignore type/lint errors in production builds, and scope image and rewrite patterns to hosts you control.

findings.code_snippet: images: { domains: ['*'] },                 // any host can be proxied
findings.message: `images.domains` contains a wildcard '*' — any host can be proxied through your Next.js image optimizer.
findings.title: Insecure Next.js configuration
findings.why: Some next.config options loosen safety in ways that are easy to ship by accident — e.g. ignoring build/type errors hides real bugs, and overly broad image `domains`/`remotePatterns` let attackers proxy arbitrary content through your domain.
findings.fix: Don't ignore type/lint errors in production builds, and scope image and rewrite patterns to hosts you control.

findings.message: This model call sets no max output-token limit — output tokens are the costliest part of an AI call, so a crafted prompt can balloon your bill. Set max_tokens / max_output_tokens.
findings.title: Model call sets no max output token limit
findings.why:
This model call doesn't set a maximum on how much text the model can
generate (no `max_tokens` / `max_output_tokens` / `maxOutputTokens`). Output
tokens are usually the most expensive part of an AI call, so an unbounded
response can cost far more than you expect — and an attacker can deliberately
craft a prompt that makes the model ramble to the limit, multiplying your
bill across many requests. It can also make responses slow and unpredictable.
findings.fix:
Set an explicit cap on output length on every model call. Pick the smallest
number that still fits your feature (e.g. a chat reply rarely needs more than
a few hundred tokens). The exact field name depends on the SDK: `max_tokens`
(OpenAI chat / Anthropic), `max_output_tokens` (OpenAI Responses), or
`maxOutputTokens` (Vercel AI SDK).

findings.message: lodash@4.17.15 is affected by CVE-2020-8203 (vulnerable <4.17.20). Upgrade to 4.17.20 or later. ✅ Lower priority: you import `lodash` but never call the vulnerable `set, setWith, zipObjectDeep`. Prototype pollution in lodash via zipObjectDeep / set-like paths allowing modification of Object.prototype.
findings.title: Dependency with a known security vulnerability
findings.why: This package version has a publicly documented security flaw (a CVE/advisory). Attackers actively scan for apps using vulnerable versions because a working exploit is already published.
findings.fix: Upgrade to the patched version listed in the advisory (`npm update <pkg>` or bump it in package.json).

metadata.summary: Prototype pollution in lodash via zipObjectDeep / set-like paths allowing modification of Object.prototype.
